text: The robotic dissection begins with an incision at the junction of the proper rectal fascia and the parietal layer of pelvic fascia, entering the pre-sacral space. The surgeon develops the pre-sacral space downward along the Holy Plane, characterized by loose aerial tissues. Posterior mobilization of the rectum continues until reaching the fourth sacral body, where a thick, tough, opaque fascia is visible, running from the periosteum over the vertebral body to the proper fascia of the rectum, approximately three to five centimeters above the anorectal junction, identified as the recto-sacral fascia.

The surgeon incises the recto-sacral fascia, entering the retro-rectal space. At the floor of this space, a dense fascia is visible covering the levator ani muscles and anococcygeal raphe, identified as the Valdius fascia.

In case number two, the surgeon performs macroscopic dissection of the recto-sacral and valdius fascia. The pre-sacral space is developed along the Holy Plane, revealing a thin membranous fibrous tissue at the level of the third sacral body, which separates the pre-sacral space into superior and inferior compartments. This tissue is identified as the recto-sacral fascia.
The surgeon cuts the recto-sacral fascia, entering the retro-rectal space. At the floor of this space, the dense Valdius fascia is visible, covering the levator ani muscle and anococcygeal raphe.

In case number three, the surgeon develops the presacral space downward to the level of the fourth sacral body. The rectal sacral fascia is visible extending from the fourth sacral body to the proper fascia of the rectum. The surgeon incises the rectal sacral fascia at the floor of the retro-rectal space, revealing the Valdias fascia, which appears more pronounced in patients after concurrent chemoradiation therapy.
